blocks.text: Chapter 2.
Trigger: What Sparks the Behavior
The ideas in 30 seconds
Every run through the Hook Model begins with a trigger — the cue that sets the behavior in motion.
External triggers carry their instruction inside them and come in four types: paid, earned, relationship, and owned.
Owned and relationship triggers drive repeat engagement; paid and earned mostly acquire new users and can't sustain a habit.
The real goal is the internal trigger, where the product becomes fused to a thought, routine, or emotion.
Negative emotions — boredom, loneliness, frustration, indecision, FOMO — are the most reliable internal triggers, firing the behavior in seconds without conscious choice.
The 5 Whys technique surfaces the underlying emotion your product actually soothes.
External Triggers Come in Four Flavors

A trigger is the actuator of behavior — the spark that tells a user what to do next. Early in a product's life, before any habit exists, that spark has to come from outside the user, and Eyal calls these external triggers. Their defining feature is that the information about what to do is embedded right in them: a button that says "Log in," a notification that says "Sara commented on your photo." The prompt and the instruction arrive together.
External triggers sort into four types, and the distinction is strategic, not academic, because they do different jobs.

Paid triggers are advertising — search ads, display, sponsored placements. They're effective but expensive, and you're renting attention. Companies use paid triggers to acquire users, but relying on them to bring users back is a losing economic proposition; you can't buy your way to a habit.

Earned triggers are the fruit of public relations and virality — a mention in the press, a featured spot in the App Store, a video that spreads. They're "free" in dollar terms but costly in effort and impossible to control. Like paid triggers, they excel at acquisition and can spike attention, but they fade and can't be summoned on demand.

Relationship triggers are one person telling or showing another — a word-of-mouth recommendation, an invitation, a shared link, a "like" that surfaces to a friend. These can be enormously powerful and can spark viral growth when a product's use naturally propagates from person to person, though the same mechanism can fuel hollow "growth hacks" that burn out.

Owned triggers are the real estate a user has agreed to let you occupy in their environment: an app icon on the home screen, an email newsletter they subscribed to, an account notification, a push alert they opted into. Owned triggers appear only with the user's consent, but once granted they let you prompt the behavior again and again at no marginal cost.

The takeaway is a division of labor: paid and earned triggers get users in the door, while owned and relationship triggers bring them back. But even a full arsenal of external triggers is a rented foundation. External triggers are training wheels — their entire purpose is to walk the user toward something more durable.
Internal Triggers Are Where Habits Actually Live

The external trigger's job is to fire often enough that it forges an association in the user's mind — until an internal trigger takes over. An internal trigger is a cue delivered by the user's own mind: a thought, a routine, or, most powerfully, an emotion. When a product becomes tightly coupled to an internal trigger, the user no longer needs a notification to prompt them. The prompt comes from inside.

Consider the mechanics. With an external trigger, the instruction of what to do is supplied by the environment. With an internal trigger, that information is encoded in the user's memory through repeated association. You feel a certain way, and reaching for the product is the automatic next step — no reminder required. This is the moment a product transitions from something you're told to use into something you reach for. It is also, not coincidentally, the moment its economics transform, because owned and earned triggers cost money and effort while an internal trigger costs the company nothing.

The strategic implication is that all the external triggering is in service of building this internal association. You run paid, earned, relationship, and owned triggers not as ends in themselves but as repetitions that wire the user's emotion to your product. Get the association to hold, and the training wheels come off.
Negative Emotions Do the Heavy Lifting

What internal triggers are strongest? Eyal's answer is uncomfortable but important: negative emotions. Feelings like boredom, loneliness, frustration, confusion, indecisiveness, and the fear of missing out are frequent, uncomfortable, and demand relief — which makes them superb engines for automatic behavior. Positive emotions can trigger use too, but the itch of a negative feeling is more insistent and more habitual.

The examples are familiar once you see the pattern. We check Instagram or Pinterest when bored. We turn to Google the instant we feel the discomfort of uncertainty or not knowing something. We open Facebook when we feel a pang of loneliness or disconnection. We refresh a feed when FOMO — the anxiety that something is happening without us — flares. In each case, an ordinary, uncomfortable emotional state arises dozens of times a day, and the product has positioned itself as the fastest way to make that discomfort go away. The user isn't deciding to use the product; they're reaching for relief, and the product is the relief.

This is why negative emotions are the designer's raw material. They occur constantly, they're mildly painful, and they create a reflexive desire to escape them. A product that becomes the go-to escape hatch for a common negative emotion has found the most durable trigger there is — one that fires many times a day, for free, from inside the user's own head. This is also precisely the mechanism worth watching in your own life: the pull you feel toward your phone is very often a negative emotion looking for the nearest exit.
Find Your Internal Trigger With the 5 Whys

Knowing that internal triggers are emotions doesn't tell you which emotion drives your specific product. To find it, Eyal borrows a technique from Toyota's manufacturing process, the 5 Whys, used to drill from a surface symptom down to a root cause. Applied to habit design, it turns a vague behavior into a specific emotional trigger you can build around.

The method is disciplined and concrete. Start by naming a real or representative user and the behavior you want them to take. Then ask "why" they would want that, and to the answer ask "why" again — five times in all. Each "why" strips away a layer of rationalization until you reach a fundamental emotion. Eyal walks through the example of a user named Julie who wants to stay in touch with colleagues: she uses email; why?; to get and share information; why?; to know what's happening; why?; to know whether her work is valued and she belongs — until the chain bottoms out at the fear of losing connection, an emotional root, not a functional one.

The point is that the useful trigger lives several layers below the obvious one. Users will tell you they want features; the 5 Whys gets you past the feature to the feeling. Design your triggers, and eventually your whole product, around that feeling — the boredom, the loneliness, the fear of not belonging — and you're building on the emotion that will one day fire the behavior on its own.
In practice
Inventory your current triggers by the four external types and confirm you're not trying to fund a habit with paid or earned triggers, which only acquire.
Invest deliberately in owned triggers (notifications, email, icon placement) and earn the permission to use them rather than assuming it.
Run the 5 Whys on a representative user to name the specific negative emotion your product relieves, then design around that emotion.
Track the shift from external to internal triggering; a maturing habit shows up as users returning without being prompted.
As a user, when you feel the urge to open an app, pause and name the emotion underneath it — boredom, loneliness, anxiety — before you tap.
Turn off nonessential owned triggers on your own devices; every push notification you disable removes a company's hook into your attention.
